data.title: Chevron: Buy And Hold Like Berkshire Hathaway
data.description:
CVX stock: Q4 earnings and BRK Q4 position

I last analyzed Chevron stock (CVX) on 1-5. That article focused on the changes in Venezuela and the potential impact on Chevron. The article rated the stock as a hold. Since then, a few new catalysts have materialized and promoted this revisit. To start, the company reported its FQ4 earnings report (ER) at the end of Jan. As you can see from the following snapshot, CVX delivered another strong quarter with a double beating. Second, Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) also released its Q4 disclosure for its equity holdings. BRK’s stock transactions are often interpreted as a market event, probably more so than the company’s ER, and for good reasons. BRK is well known for its long-term investment timeframe and an underpinning philosophy of buying durable businesses at reasonable prices.

Against this background, next I will explain why these new catalysts do help to build a compelling case for CVX as a “buy and forget” opportunity under its current conditions.

CVX stock and BRK disclosure

To wit, the following screenshot taken from Dataroma summarizes BRK’s changes to its top equity positions in the 4th quarter of 2025. The conglomerate kept selling its two largest positions. Apple (AAPL), which represents 22.60% of the portfolio, saw a trimming of about 4.3%. BRK’s 3rd-largest holding, Bank of America stock (BAC with a 10.38% weight), saw another round of trimming by about 8.9%. In contrast to these selling transactions, BRK kept adding to its CVX position, as seen with a 6.6% increase in Q4. With this addition, CVX now represents 7.24% of BRK’s total equity portfolio with a market value of almost $20 billion and ranks as the 5th largest position in BRK’s portfolio.

As a loyal follower of Warren Buffett’s investment principle (and also his portfolio changes), I have learned from experiences too that the key to investment is to approach it like a business collector or curator. Be very picky. Do not trade often. But once a wonderful target is for sale at a reasonable price, buy and hold it for a long time. To paraphrase Buffett, the preferable holding period should be forever.

Next, I will argue why Chevron indeed presents a compelling 'buy and hold forever' case under its own current conditions and also the secular trend facing the energy sector.

CVX stock: valuation and profitability metrics

Let me start with a piece of data (see the next chart below) that seems to contradict my above notion. Thanks to the share price rallies that CVX stock has enjoyed lately, Chevron is actually now one of the most expensively priced stocks among oil majors, either when compared to its domestic or foreign peers. To wit, the table below compares its FWD P/E ratios to XOM, BP, and SHEL. As seen, its FY1 P/E now is about 26.6x. This represents a valuation premium of about ¼ above XOM’s 21x P/E and roughly double the P/E ratios of BP and SHEL (around 13x to 14x).

But again, the success of investment is NOT to collect businesses that are cheaply valued but to collect the good ones at a reasonable price. Judging by the Q4 ER announced by CVX and its close peers, I consider Chevron’s valuation premium well justified by the profitability metrics (see the next chart), growth trajectory (detailed in the next section), and also its financial strength (see the second chart below). As an example, the data compares Chevron’s profitability against some of its close peers. As seen, CVX maintains far superior margins across both top-line and bottom-line metrics. Its gross profit margin of 41.83% is significantly higher than all the peers listed. In terms of bottom line, its FCF (free cash flow margin) also far exceeds all peers listed.

CVX stock: return projections

Based on the financial updates provided in CVX’s FQ4 ER, my analyses also reveal expansion of ROCE (return on capital employed) and also accelerated growth investment rates (GRR). These analyses in turn lead to good odds of double-digit annual return potential in the long term even if there is some valuation premium, as illustrated in the next chart below. The mechanism behind the chart was detailed in our earlier article, and only the end results are quoted here:

  • The long-term ROI (return on investment) for a business owner is simply determined by two things: A) the price paid to buy the business and B) the quality of the business. More specifically, part A is determined by the owner’s earning yield (“OEY”) when we purchased the business. Part B is determined by the quality of the business and that is why ROCE (return on capital employed) is the horizontal axis in the chart.
  • The long-term earnings growth rate is estimated by the product of the ROCE and the GRR.
  • Finally, long-term ROI = valuation + quality = OEY + Growth Rate = OEY + ROCE*GRR

Its current FY1 P/E of ~26.6x implies a minimum OEY of 3.75%. The operative word here is “minimum,” as CVX owners’ earnings exceed its accounting earnings. My analysis for CVX ROCE shows a TTM reading of around 28% based on Q4 updates, vs. an average of around 21% in the past 3 years. Based on the capital allocation guidance in Q4, I expect CVX’s GRR to be in the range of 25% in the next few years. As such, the organic growth rate for its profit is projected to be around 7% in my model (28% ROCE x 25% GRR). Combined with the OEY of 3.75% or better, the total annual ROI should be around 10.8% or more.

Other risks and final thoughts

In terms of downside risks, BRK disclosures in recent quarters have likely generated some “Buffett premium” on CVX stock. As mentioned earlier, during Q4 of 2025, Berkshire increased its stake by 6.63%, adding approximately 8.09 million shares to bring its total to 130.16 million shares at a reported price of around $152. Before that, BRK also disclosed another addition of 2.91% in Q2 2025 at a reported price of around $143. Currently, the share prices hover around $180 as of this writing, significantly above BRK’s average entry prices. As shown in the chart above, under the assumptions I made above, the ROI at BRK’s average entry price is around 11.7%, noticeably above the expected ROI under its current conditions.

To wrap up, this CVX reexamination was primarily motivated by two new catalysts since my last writing: its Q4 ER and also the BRK disclosure of its equity changes made in Q4. These catalysts, when viewed together, have led me to see a “buy-and-hold” opportunity for CVX shares. Like many other BRK equity investments in the past, I see good odds for CVX to deliver annual returns (in terms of total shareholder returns) in the double digits in the long term.

data.title: Whirlpool: Fairly Valued As Stock Begins 2026 Outperforming
data.description:
Shares in appliance maker Whirlpool (WHR) have rebounded since an underwhelming Q4 earnings release, which included declining sales on weak volume in its North and Latin American markets.

Following an initial decline upon the release, shares are up about 3% over the past month and are now up over 20% YTD. This compares to flat performance from the broader S&P 500 (SPY) over the same period.

With shares at the midpoint of its 52-week range and at a very reasonable trading multiple, I believe the company’s stock is worth the attention. In January, consumer confidence hit a 12-year low, and this was called out by CEO Marc Bitzer in the Q4 earnings release.

While confidence hasn’t exactly improved much since then, I do expect the slump to be transitory. Any rebound should provide some momentum to WHR, which already is expecting organic net sales growth of 5% in 2026. While I don’t necessarily believe shares are a ‘screaming buy’ just yet, I do believe it’s worth the follow.

WHR Stock Key Metrics

Shares in WHR appear reasonably valued based on its current forward GAAP earnings multiple of about 15x. This is both below the sector average of 19x and WHR’s own five-year average in the low 20x range. The stock also appears discounted through the lens of other metrics as well. At just 0.3x its sales, for example, WHR is well below the 1x sector and its own historical averages.

The relative valuation of WHR is its strongest mark in the Seeking Alpha (“SA”) factor grades. Shares also maintain a positive mark on momentum, and this is likely due to WHR’s YTD gain so far of about 20%.

The quants, overall, are neutral, and this echoes the sentiment among both the SA analyst community and those on Wall Street.

Based on the most current estimated average price target, shares look to be valued appropriately in today’s environment, with the consensus seeing less than 2% upside from current levels.

WHR Q4 Operating Performance

WHR slogged through Q4, ending what was a very difficult year for the appliance maker. For the full 2025 year, for example, global organic revenue was essentially flat, and Q4 reflected many of the same pressures seen earlier in the year.

In its most important segment, North America continued to face an intense promotional environment as the industry was slow to pass along tariff-related cost increases. That ultimately weighed on profitability, with MDA North America posting a Q4 EBIT margin of just 2.8% and a full-year margin of roughly 5%.

The tariff dynamic was a key aspect and theme of WHR’s results throughout 2025. Whirlpool absorbed roughly +$300M of tariff costs in 2025 while competitors delayed pricing adjustments. This created a margin squeeze for WHR that was especially pronounced in the back half of the year.

On a positive note, the management team noted that promotional intensity began to ease in December and into early 2026. This could perhaps be an inflection point, in my view.

There were bright spots within the Q4 release as well. Latin America remained profitable despite macro volatility, and the Small Domestic Appliances (‘SDA’) business, led by KitchenAid, delivered standout results with approximately 10% Q4 sales growth and a strong 16% full-year EBIT margin.

All considered, I believe the main takeaway for Q4 and the full year was that WHR’s business remained resilient in a tough environment heavily dominated by tariff-related challenges.

What Is The Outlook For WHR Stock?

Looking ahead, I expect 2026 to bring continued rebuilding, as well as the possibility of margin expansion.

For 2026, WHR expects revenue growth of roughly 5%, supported by new product launches in North America and continued strength in its SDA and Latin America businesses.

Importantly, guidance does not assume a meaningful rebound in discretionary demand tied to existing home sales. In my view, there could be better-than-expected upside if housing begins to thaw faster than expected.

From a profitability standpoint, Whirlpool is targeting 80–110 basis points of EBIT margin expansion, bringing full-year margins into the 5.5%–5.8% range. The improvement is expected to come from price/mix benefits tied to new product launches, normalization in promotional activity, and more than +$150M in incremental cost reductions. That said, tariffs will still be a headwind in the first half of the year, representing roughly 125 basis points of margin pressure.

The EPS guide was set at the $7/share range, alongside a notable step-up in FCF to +$400–$500M. Overall, I believe the outlook suggests stabilization and gradual improvement. That said, much of the upside still hinges on housing and consumer confidence trends, in my view.

Is WHR Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

At current levels, I view shares in WHR as a hold.

The stock has already staged a meaningful rebound to start 2026, up more than 20% YTD and outperforming the broader market. After a prolonged stretch of selling pressure, that move was warranted, in my opinion.

The stock now trades at roughly 15x forward earnings. While this is below both the sector average and its own historical multiple, I do not view this as a level that screams deep value given the still-muted demand environment.

Operationally, the business appears to be stabilizing. Promotional intensity is easing, pricing actions are taking hold, and management is guiding to margin expansion and improved FCF. The SDA segment also remains a bright spot, and cost actions should help offset tariff pressures as the year progresses.

That said, I believe WHR remains highly tethered to housing turnover and consumer sentiment, both of which are still slumping. It’s worth noting that forward guidance isn’t baking in a housing rebound, and I think that could be a key catalyst for better-than-expected results. If existing home sales begin to recover meaningfully, there could be upside to both earnings and valuation, in my view.

For now, however, with consensus price targets implying limited upside and shares fairly valued, I believe WHR is best kept on ‘hold.’

data.title: With A High Yield And Mounting Pressures, TELUS' CEO Switch Raises Big Questions For Investors
data.description:
Author's note: All figures are listed in Canadian dollars.

CEO Succession

On February 12, 2026, Telus Corporation (T:CA) announced that after 26 years, Chief Executive Officer Darren Entwistle would be stepping down. This news comes as an unexpected change following a December 2025 announcement that Entwistle would spend at least one more year in the role. The top job will be assumed by former CIBC Inc. (CM:CA) CEO Victor Dodig, who has served on Telus’ board of directors since 2022. This veteran banker will begin work on the leadership team on May 1 and officially commence work in the CEO role as of July 1, 2026. Entwistle will stay on in an advisory role as CEO emeritus until April 30, 2027.

Market reaction to the announcement is difficult to parse, as the company released earnings on the same day. The market reaction to Telus’ Q4 2025 and Full year earnings resulted in a 3% decline in the share price on Thursday, February 12, 2026, with Telus missing Q4 earnings and revenue expectations. Shares of the company are down 14% over the past 12 months.

As the new chief executive of Canada's worst-performing large telecom in 2025, Dodig has a number of immediate challenges. The company is struggling to grow its core earnings, preparing to monetize its health division, and advancing a deleveraging plan. Income-seeking investors will be watching with interest what the new CEO appointment means for Telus' dividend, currently yielding more than 9%.

Entwistle’s Tenure

Darren Entwistle has held the CEO role since 2000, with the exception of a one-year period in 2015 when the Board Appointed Joe Natale to the role and moved Entwistle to Executive Chairman. Telus has undergone a significant transformation as a company since Entwistle started his term as CEO in 2000. In 1999, only 22% of Canadians used mobile devices. At the beginning of Entwistle’s tenure as CEO, Telus earned 6% of revenue from advertising in yellow and white phone books, and revenues from the wireless segment accounted for less revenue than the company collected from long-distance charges.

Over Entwistle’s 26 years, the company established itself as one of Canada’s three main wireless providers and built out large related businesses in health, agriculture, and digital services. To cement Telus’ position as the main provider of wireline internet and TV services, Entwistle oversaw a capital-intensive build-out of its fibre-to-the-home network and mobile tower infrastructure system that saw the company spending a staggering $276B on Canadian network infrastructure, operations, and spectrum over the last 24 years.

Succession Context

Until 2022, Telus was a stock market darling. The company earned a premium valuation relative to its Canadian telecom peers and boasted an incredible record of dividend increases. These dividend increases were executed twice annually for the majority of Entwistle’s tenure. From a share price high in April 2022, Telus stock has given up 46% of its value from its peak of over $34 per share.

This leadership succession comes at a critical time for Telus, as the company has struggled with growth, high leverage, and questions about the sustainability of its dividend. Amid the news of Telus’ CEO change, the company reported mixed results.

On the Q4 2025 results announced on February 12, 2026, Telus reported profit attributable to shareholders of $292M, or $0.19 per share, falling short of the consensus estimate of $0.25. This compared to $358M, or $0.24 per share, in the same quarter in 2024. Operating revenue totaled $5.26B in Q4 2025, down 2.3% from $5.38B in the fourth quarter of 2024. This result was also short of the $5.4B expected by analysts.

Full-year 2025 results were more positive, with operating revenue up 1% over 2024 to $20.3B. While adjusted EBITDA was flat, free cash flow increased 11% year over year to $2.2B. Capex for the period was up 1%; however, Telus has targeted a 10% decrease in capital expenditures in 2026. Declining capital spending and EBITDA growth targets of 2-4% in 2026 will support the company’s goal of growing free cash flow by an additional 10% for the year.

Four Key Challenges Facing Telus’ New CEO

Growth and Profitability

Compared to other Canadian telecoms, Telus has made considerable investments diversifying into other industries. While these areas have experienced solid growth, building scale in businesses like health care and agriculture has required significant acquisitions. Some of these large investments for the health division include $763M for Emergis Inc., $2.9B for LifeWorks, and $500M for Workplace Options. These large investments have been a significant draw on capital and contributed to indebtedness.

Despite the cost, these divisions have delivered growth. For FY 2025, fixed data service revenue was up 3% over the previous year, while wireless revenue was flat. Over the same period, Telus Health EBITDA grew 22%. Fast-growing segments such as health care have led to improving margins. The current trailing 12-month EBITDA margin of 28% is 170 bp ahead of the company’s 5-year average.

This growth in non-telecom industries has been especially important during this period of intense price competition in the Canadian wireless market. Average Revenue Per User, or "ARPU", a key operating performance metric, declined 2.8% in 2025 to $57.01.

Monetization of Telus Health

According to the Financial Post, analysts expected that a leadership change would only come about following the anticipated spin-off of Telus’ health division. According to TD Securities analyst Vince Valentini, “We thought this might come only after a monetization of Telus Health.” The spin-off of Telus Health has long been championed by Entwistle. In January 2026, the company announced progress towards this monetization effort through the appointment of TD Securities Inc. and Jefferies Securities, Inc. as financial advisors to:

Assist with strategy and timeline for the monetisation of its Telus Health business, which may include the identification and evaluation of potential strategic partnerships, including evaluation of all partnership structures.

Telus Health is the company’s most important non-core telecom business. The health division provides digital health services to more than 160 million people across 200 countries and territories. In the first three quarters of 2025, the division generated year-to-date operating revenue of $1.5B with EBITDA of $258M. Over the last year, Telus Health has digested some of its recent acquisitions, including achieving $431M in annualized synergies following the $2.3B purchase of LifeWorks in 2022.

According to Liam Gallagher, analyst at Veritas Investment Research, Telus Health could be valued at $5B-$6B. With FY 2025 EBITDA of $322M, this would imply a valuation of approximately 17x EBITDA. This implied value may be generous based on the 15x EBITDA Telus paid for LifeWorks in 2022. After being burned on the collapse of Telus International's share value in the company's most recent spin-off, investors will want to ensure that the company creates shareholder value through the Telus Health transaction.

Should Dodig successfully complete a transaction for Telus Health, the company will need to demonstrate that growth can be achieved through the company's core telecom business. While Telus has continued to grow its wireline and mobile connections, with nearly 1.1 million new connections in 2025, telecom ARPU has been under pressure for several years.

Deleveraging

After years of growth by acquisitions and a capital-intensive fibre network build-out, Telus is looking to repair its balance sheet. As recently as January 2026, the company announced its commitment to ongoing deleveraging:

Telus is progressing ahead of plan, with 2025 net debt to adjusted EBITDA currently projected at approximately 3.4-times, as we aim to reach circa 3.3-times or lower by year-end 2026, and 3.0-times by the end of 2027.

As part of the company’s deleveraging efforts, Telus has been issuing hybrid debt. While the bond ratings agencies consider these to have a 50% equity weighting, they tend to carry higher interest expenses than the company’s traditional debt. As of the most recent quarter, trailing twelve-month interest expense has doubled over the last five years, from $719M in 2020 to $1.455B in 2025. Over the same period, net debt has increased from $17.7B to $24.9B.

Dividend Sustainability

One of the key questions investors are trying to ascertain as Dodig takes the helm is what does this change in leadership mean for the dividend? As the new CEO, Dodig inherits a company that recently announced a pause to dividend increases, reversing course after years of steady dividend increases. On the company’s Q4 2025 earnings call, outgoing CEO Darren Entwistle addressed the company’s elevated dividend payout ratio:

Based on the current quarterly dividend, our cash dividend payout ratio is approximately 70% on a prospective basis, and we anticipate that it will remain in the 70s over the course of our multiyear plan with the net effect of our deleveraging plan in action and the DDRIP removal. Resuming dividend growth will be contingent upon maintaining this payout ratio trajectory with the DDRIP discount fully removed as we execute against our strategic plan, including free cash flow generation and achieving our deleveraging target.

The company targets a long-term dividend payout ratio of 60 to 75% of free cash flow on a prospective basis. While Entwistle notes the company is paying out approximately 70% of free cash flow, this ratio is likely to increase as the DRIP discount is stepped down. Fewer investors will have an incentive to reinvest shares. Under the current program, Telus pays out approximately $800M in shares annually through its DRIP out of the total gross dividend obligation of approximately $2.5B. While removing the DRIP discount will ultimately be positive to curb the dilutive effect of the DRIP plan, in the short term, it means that more investors will opt for cash dividends.

At current levels, Telus has a trailing twelve-month dividend payout ratio (Non-GAAP) of 179%, approximately 25% higher than its 5-year average.

Under Entwistle, dividend growth was an implicit part of the company’s investor appeal, with 36 dividend increases since 2004. With the appointment of a veteran banker, Victor Dodig, investors and analysts are assessing the ongoing commitment to the dividend. According to Bank of Nova Scotia analyst Maher Yaghi:

Under Darren’s leadership we were operating under the assumption that the dividend is sacred and will be defended. The board with a new CEO might have other prerogatives on capital allocation.

Similar sentiments were made clear by Bank of Montreal analyst Tim Casey:

We think a dividend cut and asset sales or business exits (Health, Agriculture) are more likely under new leadership.

While not specifically referencing the dividend, TD Securities analyst Vince Valentini opined that Dodig may have other capital allocation priorities:

We suspect many shareholders will welcome fresh leadership on strategy and capital allocation.

In terms of capital allocation, there is a strong case for stepping up share buybacks as a vehicle for returning capital to shareholders. With the share prices depressed, Telus could create value by cancelling shares, boosting EPS, and reducing its dividend expense burden. Following asset sales for Telus Health and potentially other non-core businesses, Dodig may also pursue M&A opportunities with the proceeds. Ahead of BCE’s dividend cut last year, the company pursued the purchase of U.S. fibre operator Ziply as a new growth strategy.

Investor Takeaways

While many investors will be pleased to see new leadership at Telus, the new CEO has a myriad of challenges to address. As the company advances multiple concurrent goals, investors will be looking to assess how Victor Dodig balances monetizing non-core assets with driving growth. With a commitment to deleveraging, the new CEO will make his mark on how capital is best allocated to maximize shareholder value. Telus shares are currently yielding over 9%, suggesting a decision about the sustainability of the dividend will be one of Dodig's first key tests with shareholders and an indication of the new CEO's strategic priorities.

Given the challenges the company faces, I continue to rate Telus a hold. Clarity on the dividend policy and indications of continued growth in the core telecom segment would be thresholds for me to consider upgrading. Executing the Telus Health transaction and achieving deleveraging plans will be tangible milestones to regain investor confidence in the coming quarters.

data.title: Stay Cautious On Algoma Steel, But Watch For Early Signs Of Recovery
data.description:
A Hold Rating Is What We Need At The Moment For Algoma Steel

We still see a “Hold” rating as the more viable rating for the NASDAQ-listed shares of Algoma Steel Group Inc. (ASTL) as we were with our previous article, but the underlying reasons are different. These reasons are now funding ASTL's updated vision that, however, things are moving in the cycle, require an enhanced level of attention to this stock's prospects.

From Our Previous Coverage

The article reaffirmed a Hold rating for Algoma Steel Group Inc., as the company struggled due to weak U.S. steel demand, a global economic slowdown, trade tariffs, and geopolitical uncertainty. The company was experiencing declining revenues, negative profit margins and had to suspend its dividend payments. The company faced significant financial pressures, including high leverage and weak cash flow, and analysts warned of a high risk of default based on its debt-to-EBITDA ratio and Altman Z-score. The company was exploring liquidity options, including government borrowing, but the potential delay was a concern due to its impact on the conversion of its electric arc furnace, which shareholders have high expectations for, and long-term prospects for, this promising project.

Until more clarity on the economic situation, the Federal Reserve’s rate stance, and the company’s ability to secure liquidity, caution was advised in an uncertain environment.

Reasons To Maintain A Hold Rating

Despite the same rating, Hold, shares of the Canadian steel producer have fundamentals that have seen certain geopolitical developments turn in their favor. Nevertheless, the overall environment remains challenging, too much to withstand the ultimate hesitation and unconditionally rely on a recovery in business confidence. US manufacturing companies continue to face weak demand, and the very slow employment growth also suggests that spring has not yet come. Steel producers are reducing staff as they see their profit margins threatened. In early December 2025, Algoma also announced a drastic reduction of 1,000 jobs—

one of the biggest employment hits to date for Canada—amid a struggling domestic industry, as reported by Seeking Alpha.

This could not be solely a reaction to Trump's tariffs, which, while a serious measure, always had only temporary effects. Rather, it is a suspicion that the difficult period is not yet over, with its reluctance to recall the headwinds that have plagued the North American steel industry. And to defend margins, producers like Algoma are raising selling prices as they try to pass on the increase in production costs to their customers. They have been raising

their output charges to the greatest degree since March 2025. (says Trading Economics),

but despite these efforts, “Steel Rebar Futures Prices” were at their three-month low in February.

At the time of writing, Trading Economics reported on production bottlenecks in China's steel industry, the world's largest steel producer. These bottlenecks may have supported steel prices on global markets, thus helping Canadian and US steel exporters offset weak demand caused by the trade war and ongoing uncertainties.

In fact, Trading Economics notes:

Steel rebar futures steadied above CNY 3,050 per ton in mid-February, but remained close to three-month lows as trading in mainland China paused for the Lunar New Year holiday from Feb. 16 to 23. Chinese mills scaled back operations amid a seasonal slowdown, with both blast furnaces and electric arc furnaces undergoing scheduled maintenance. Heavy air pollution alerts in parts of Hebei have also raised the likelihood of temporary output restrictions. Meanwhile, data from the World Steel Association indicated that global steel production declined in 2025, largely due to output cuts in China under its anti-involution campaign. China remained the world’s largest steel producer, with output totaling 960.8 Mt. last year, followed by India, the US, Japan, and Russia, which together produced 395.4 Mt.

A Note: Regarding the weak demand in the US and Canadian manufacturing industries, the website of Trading Economics is reporting on the S&P Global US Manufacturing PMI and the S&P Global Canada Manufacturing PMI, respectively, at the time of this writing.

But if this steel price level represents the maximum opportunity for steel producers to capitalize on, what about the seasonal nature of Chinese production restrictions as catalysts? That is, what if, when these restrictions are lifted? We mean the implications for profit margins and investor sentiment around Algoma Steel’s shares. If steel demand, as suggested by data from the World Steel Association’s global steel production figures, continues to decline in 2025, what kind of deduction will that lead to for aggregate demand? Especially given the seventh consecutive monthly decline in export orders in the US manufacturing industry (as Trading Economics reports on the S&P Global US Manufacturing PMI as of this writing). This decline is largely attributed to ongoing uncertainty around trade, particularly the impact of the Trump-era tariffs. While these tariffs weighing on Canadian steel and aluminum appear set to be rolled back by the US president, uncertainty remains, exacerbated by ongoing macroeconomic and geopolitical turmoil.

The $500 million in funding from the governments of Canada and Ontario will address some of the solvency concerns raised in our previous reports and mitigate the risk of disruption to the ongoing electric arc furnace conversion. But if the company has not yet found the necessary strength on its feet, in addition to this significant financial support, it suggests that the current market conditions are still very difficult to cope with. The Canadian steelmaker needs help, and that's why we still think this Hold rating is appropriate as a cautionary signal for Algoma Steel stock.

Algoma Steel Group, As Of Q3 2025: The Situation

Due to the aforementioned difficulties, Algoma Steel Group Inc. had a challenging third quarter of 2025, as reflected in the key financials.

Year-over-year, the following changes occurred in the key financial figures:

Consolidated Revenue: Decrease of 12.7% to C$523.9 million.

Consolidated Operating Loss: Increase (deterioration) of 679.3%, including a non-cash impairment charge of C$503.4 million and an increase of 77.2% excluding this charge.

Note: The impairment charge of C$ 503.4 million was determined after the recoverable amount of the cash-generating unit (the assets that can generate income) was exceeded by the carrying amount of the assets ("the net assets") and after considering the effects of US tariffs under Section 232.

If we exclude the direct tariff costs of $89.7 million in the third quarter of 2025 (compared to zero in the prior year's quarter), the lower market valuation of the cash-generating unit of the Algoma Steel Group, in order to also convey the impression of the unit's reduced ability to generate inflows at present, represents 82% of the total impairment loss.

Net loss: Increase (deterioration) of 355% to C$485.1 million.

Adjusted EBITDA: A negative shift to a loss of C$87.1 million in Q3 2025, from a profit of C$3.5 million in Q3 2024.

The adjusted EBITDA margin deteriorated to a negative margin of -16.6% in Q3 2025, from a positive margin of +0.6% in Q3 2024.

Operating cash flow: It was C$117.3 million (outflow) in Q3 2025, compared to a cash inflow of C$25.5 million in Q3 2024. This was a deterioration as well.

Shipments decreased by 19.5% to 419,173 tons in Q3 2025.

Short- And Long-Term Strategy For The Reintroduction Of Sustainable Dividends For Shareholders

In July 2025, the board suspended the regular quarterly dividend payment to safeguard liquidity. Investors can assume that this will only resume once the company is largely profitable again, and this could take some time. To achieve this, the company's strategy for successfully navigating challenging market conditions focuses on discrete plate production for domestic demand and reducing coil production to mitigate the impact of tariffs. But with the US government now expected to lift the tariffs on Canadian steel and aluminum imports, Algoma may already be rethinking its strategy and realigning its market presence to improve profitability. Decommissioning its blast furnace and coke oven facilities and replacing them with low-carbon steel production at its new electric arc furnace plant (EAF facility) is a strategy more focused on long-term sustainability.

In his commentary on the results for the third quarter of 2025, Michael Garcia, CEO of Algoma Steel Group, said:

In response to current market dynamics, we are accelerating our transition to EAF steelmaking, expediting our evolution into one of North America's lowest-cost green steel producers. While we cannot control macro challenges or market access issues, we remain focused on what we can control, the successful execution of our transformation strategy. We are confident that the flexibility and structural cost advantages we are building through our investment in green steelmaking technology will serve us well across market cycles and create lasting value for all stakeholders.

Trump's Tariff Rollback Not Factored In Algoma's Q4 2025 Guidance

Seeking Alpha reports:

 Algoma Steel Group (ASTL) (ASTL:CA) expects adjusted EBITDA in the range of negative C$95M to negative C$105M for the fourth quarter of 2025.

 Total steel shipments for the quarter are expected to be in the range of 375K to 380K tons.

Rajat Marwah, CEO of Algoma, commented on the Guidance for the Fourth Quarter of 2025:

Our fourth-quarter results were in line with expectations, reflecting the continued impact of steel tariffs and the previously announced wind-down of our blast furnace operations, which are expected to conclude in the coming days.

We are encouraged by the ongoing ramp-up of the first unit of our Electric Arc Furnace (“EAF”) project, which is now operating 6 days per week, and we remain on schedule with the second unit. As we move toward completing our transition to EAF steelmaking during the current quarter, we continue to optimize our existing assets and advance discussions with potential partners to expand our finishing capabilities.

This strategy is deliberately aligned with Canada’s national interest—strengthening domestic steelmaking capacity, supporting critical infrastructure and defence supply chains, and reinforcing Canada’s long-term industrial competitiveness.

ASTL Algoma Steel Group Inc. On The NASDAQ

Shares of Algoma Steel Group Inc., which trade on the NASDAQ under the symbol “ASTL,” were quoted at $4.58 per share, representing a market capitalization of $486.89 million.

Technical Analysis

The stock price has fluctuated between $3.02 and $8.23 per share over the past 52 weeks. The average of this range is $5.625 per share. Currently, Algoma Steel Group's stock is trading 18.6% below this 52-week average. The 20-day moving average is above the 50-day moving average, suggesting that the relationship between the two has normalized since mid-December 2025. This suggests that sentiment toward Algoma shares is gradually calming in the short term, possibly because the trade war is no longer as prominent in market news and relations between economies, particularly between the US and Canada, are somewhat more relaxed than before. However, the 200-day moving average remains well above the short-term lines, indicating that the market does not expect a sustained long-term upward trend. Therefore, any recent recovery in the stock price could be merely a short-term effect, possibly driven by volatility. This is consistent with a Hold recommendation, as is the RSI of 51-52, which has been in the neutral range for some time.

The Valuation

Based on Algoma Steel Group's projected adjusted EBITDA for the fourth quarter of 2025 (between -C$95 million and -C$105 million), and even assuming a gradual return to some profitability, it is unlikely that the company will be able to position itself better than the SECTOR median EV/EBITDA (FWD) of 9.82x in the short term. It will take some time before the market signals attractive valuations for Algoma Steel Group's stock. The current enterprise value is US$1.02 billion, which means the FWD-EBITDA estimate will need to be revised upwards to at least US$103.9 million or C$141.8 million. There will be no shortage of capital to restore profitability to the distressed assets—at least, that is the intention of the Canadian government. But the path from a very negative to a very positive profitability forecast is long and requires a strategy that is still waiting to see how things develop. This is our vision.

Risk/Reward

An Altman Z-score of 0.55 indicates a balance sheet in acute financial distress and a high risk of insolvency within a few years (see Seeking Alpha’s “Risk” section).

As of September 30, 2025, the company had total cash and cash equivalents of C$337.1 million, consisting of C$4.5 million in cash and cash equivalents and C$332.6 million from its ABL credit line. But as the company draws on the credit line, the available amount becomes a loan to be repaid. As of September 30, 2025, the total debt amounted to C$745.1 million, of which C$84.1 million was interest the company paid in the preceding twelve months. The assets are not generating income to cover interest expenses but are instead causing losses. Therefore, there is a risk that the debt will lead to further debt unless the company quickly becomes profitable and starts to create value.

The press release dated November 17, 2025, regarding the completion of a C$500 million government financing transaction by Algoma Steel Group, states:

[…]. Algoma has issued 6.77 million common share purchase warrants to CEEFC and Ontario proportionately, with each warrant being exercisable for one common share of Algoma at an exercise price of $11.08 for a 10-year term, vesting proportionately as unsecured draws are made under each of the CEEFC and Ontario facilities.

Here’s what we’re saying: Let’s leave the risk of Algoma Steel’s hoped-for recovery to the Canadian government. They have agreed to lend the impressive sum of C$500 million in tranches to restructure the company and accelerate the decarbonization of its steel production. C$500 million is a significant amount that, if used wisely and strategically, should gradually reduce corporate risk while laying the foundation for long-term, sustainable growth. We already know that the stock can be acquired in the future for C$11.08 by exercising a warrant option. At the current exchange rate, this equates to approximately US$8.12 per share, representing a potential upside of 75.8% from the current market price. When the strategy delivers the desired results and the Canadian government gradually disburses the additional tranches, this will signal that the company is back on track. Even before the company resumes its dividend policy—a clear sign of recovery and a return to prosperity—investors are likely to change their strategy from “Hold” to “Buy.” This opens up a potential share price increase of 75.8%, which will be further amplified by the sentiment generated by dividend enthusiasm. Since dividends are only paid when profits are generated—the most important factor influencing the sentiment of the US stock market—and this relationship has been confirmed by the facts over time, dividends, as the ultimate proof that a company is creating value and returning it to the market, will inevitably revive demand for Algoma Steel Group shares.

Conclusion

Algoma Steel Group Inc., a leading Canadian producer of hot- and cold-rolled steel sheets, continues to feel the effects of the trade conflict. Some mitigation of these effects could be achieved through the lifting of tariffs on Canadian products by US President Trump and the simultaneous acceleration of the conversion of electric arc furnaces in Canada. The business environment is under considerable pressure in terms of revenue, and market conditions remain too challenging for a rapid recovery. Nevertheless, the government seems to have confidence in Algoma's long-term prospects for producing sustainable steel with a lower carbon footprint and will provide a substantial loan of CAD 500 million in several tranches. Investors should remain cautious for the time being and wait to see if the first tranches of this capital mark the beginning of a new phase. In the meantime, market conditions may improve, and as you sense a reactivation of dividends, you can take steps to anticipate the enthusiasm this will generate for Algoma shares on the Nasdaq, and then it may be time to upgrade your opinion on this stock again. This may take some time, and for now, it is best to exercise caution.

data.title: Viavi: No Longer A Telecom Cycle Story
data.description:
Last December, I argued that Viavi Solutions Inc. (VIAV) was becoming more structurally durable. Now, the company’s results for Q2 FY26 are out, and we can see some tangible progress around its growth drivers. The quarter was a good one by several metrics (revenue and margins are up), and its mix is moving decisively toward data centers as well as aerospace & defense.

The market has also rewarded the stock (it’s up ~40% since my December publication), and I don’t think that it has fully repriced. I see more room for Viavi’s earnings power to compound from here, but it’s going to take more than another good quarter to push the stock even further.

Q2 FY26 Results: This Was Not a Normal Quarter

Metric

QoQ

+36.4%

+45.8%
