The headline figures were fantastic, but the underlying numbers are just as important. For example, the company recorded an operating income increase of 77% over the same period in fiscal 2025, which is why EPS rose by about 70%. The incremental revenue is coming in with much better structural margins, and you can see it clearly in the operating margins.

In terms of the business segments, we have more of the same: NSE is doing the heavy lifting at 80% of revenue. The good part is that it is growing quickly, too, so the topline is benefiting heavily here. Conversely, OSP isn’t growing anywhere near the same pace, and it actually recorded a negative growth rate compared to Q1 FY26.

Then, we have gross margins. Viavi’s GAAP gross margin was 60.4%, but its non-GAAP gross margin was closer to 62%. I’d say this is worth a pause because it’s been rising steadily over the last few quarters, and it looks like we might see a stabilization around the low-to-mid 60s range in the next few quarters.

Operating margin is also another talking point because it is now at 19+%. Historically speaking, the business has been in the mid-teens, and if we see the margin jump sustained in the high teens to low 20s, that materially changes how we might start valuing Viavi. For me, the business is looking more and more like a high-value instrumentation and software profile, and I believe it has a sustainable margin expansion story from here.

Besides the numbers, we have word from management that they want to restructure the business and cut the workforce by 5% “to improve operational efficiencies and better align workforce and resources with our current business needs and strategic priorities.” They plan to complete the reduction by the end of 2026 and spend around $32 million doing so. Apparently, the net benefit should be ~$30 million annually once it is done.

On top of that, the company is progressing with the synergies from the Spirent acquisition, something I’ve covered in more detail in my previous articles. While all of that is happening, we won’t see any capital returns via share buybacks either, because management wants to reduce the debt load on the business.

On the one hand, I understand why they are doing so much at the same time to cut costs. After all, we all like a more profitable business, and profitability will only help to boost investor confidence. On the other hand, I can’t quite get behind the planned layoffs, mostly because of the human element. Besides, sacking 5% of your workforce is unlikely to save you an additional $30 million annually if total employee compensation is around $335 million in the same period. They won’t be sending senior managers and executives packing, at least not while integrating a new business, so it’s the lower-level employees who will likely feel the pinch more than most. Anyway, I suppose there will be cost savings across other parts of the business, so we’ll just have to wait and see how the staff reduction pans out.

All things considered, I think it’s clear that they’re not assuming peak demand just yet, and keeping margins high is a priority. As such, I see the 19% operating margin as a potential new baseline.

The Mix Shift Is No Longer Subtle

One thing I’ve been watching closely with Viavi is whether the revenue mix will actually change in a way that reduces cyclicality, and Q2 gives me more confidence that it has.

Let’s look at the exposure clearly: about 45% data center, 40% service provider, and 15% aerospace & defense. The majority of the company’s revenue now sits in end markets that are growing structurally. In my view, the data center piece is the most important. There’s a lot of sustained demand for instrumentation because of hyperscalers, AI-related infrastructure upgrades, and faster network transitions.

During the Q2 earnings call, management also mentioned that it has extended visibility into revenue pipelines. Now, it’s roughly three quarters, compared to one to one-and-a-half quarters historically. I like this newfound visibility because it gives us a clearer view of what to expect from Viavi over an extended period, and that’s not a small detail.

The aerospace and defense segment of the company’s customer base is still small, but 15% is a very meaningful number. We must remember that the segment adds program-based revenue that tends to be stickier and less volatile. I know it won’t drive headline growth, but I appreciate how it improves Viavi’s stability profile.

Finally, we have service provider exposure. It’s still pulling its weight for sure, but clearly, it’s no longer the dominant variable in the model. That’s the real shift for me.

Valuation

It’s a tad more difficult for me to justify a Buy at this price, given how out of sync with the fundamentals the stock seems to be. However, I’m positive that there’s more value to unlock, even if the underlying numbers need to catch up to the current multiples.

  • Share price: $26.5

  • Market cap: $6.08 billion

  • Total debt: $1.33 billion

  • Cash: $767.4 million

  • Enterprise value: $6.65 billion

I’m going to annualize the company’s EPS using $0.23 per quarter. That comes out to ~$0.92 for FY26. When we compare that to its non-GAAP forward P/E of 31.45x, it’s obvious that this is an expensive stock, especially for a company that’s still struggling to be profitable on a GAAP basis. The same goes for its EV/EBITDA multiple, which sits at 21x.

Viavi’s historical range for these multiples is much lower: low 20s for the P/E and low teens for EV/EBITDA. That was when it was primarily a telecom-focused business that was struggling with mid-teen operating margins. Today, we have those fundamentals in a much better place, and at this price, I’d say the expensive multiples are just about right. I will admit that they’re close to the limit for me.

We could say that they’re justified if the margins hold and we see the ATS-style durability, but this isn’t a deep value stock anymore. At the same time, it would be hard to argue against another re-rating if Viavi’s margin base stabilizes in the low 20s and its EPS expands toward $1.10–1.20 over the next 18–24 months. That would leave us with a 22-24x EV/EBITDA multiple, and the price could get into the low to mid 30s range at that point. It’s not impossible, but I wouldn’t bank everything on it.

Risks

I have a number of reservations about the stock’s price at this level, but the market hasn’t exactly considered those over the last few weeks. Even Wall Street analysts have Viavi listed as a Strong Buy, and it all seems a bit too rosy. So, I will highlight what I see as the main risks to a bullish position on the stock.

The first risk is that data center demand cools faster than expected. A lot of the recent acceleration that we saw in the Q2 earnings report is tied to hyperscale buildouts and AI-related infrastructure upgrades. This means that if that cycle normalizes abruptly, it is likely that the company’s revenue growth will slow down quickly while the market is still pricing in durability.

Next, there’s the margin sustainability. Operating margin at ~19–20% looks like a new baseline, and I’m confident that it is achievable in the near term. However, it is also possible that mixed shifts stall or restructuring savings underdeliver, and if these happen, the business could drift back toward the mid-teens profile investors remember. At 31x forward earnings, there just isn’t much room for margin disappointment.

The Bar Is Much Higher Now

Viavi is a very different company from what it was a year or two ago, and we can see that in the numbers and the valuation. The company’s outlook is very promising, and I like the look of things moving forward. At the same time, getting in at this price means that you’re willing to accept a sizable amount of downside risk, although I think there’s still a fair bit of upside.

The important thing from here is how the company executes against its own guidance and investors’ expectations. That performance largely depends on things like data center demand and cost discipline, and I believe that as long as it can work with those, we will see its earnings power continue to expand. Consequently, I’m issuing a rather cautious Buy recommendation.

data.title: Vanda Pharma: High Risk, Heavily Discounted - Stock Ahead Of PDUFA This Week
data.description:
Thesis

Vanda Pharmaceuticals Inc. (VNDA) disappointed the market with a pretty weak 4Q25. They reported a GAAP EPS of -$2.39, which missed consensus by about $1.07, and a revenue figure of $57.22 million that came in about $2.05 million below expectations despite revenue growing 9% year over year. The earnings miss was driven mostly by some hefty operating expenses and a big non-cash tax valuation allowance. This didn't make for good viewing for investors and only raised more concerns about near-term profitability and cash burn. The good side was that management reaffirmed 2026 revenue to fall in the range of $230 to $260 million.

The day of earnings, the stock fell about 11% after hours and has been on the downward slope since. I think there's a lot of investor frustration from the fact that there is clearly strong Fanapt growth and pipeline progress, but the sheer magnitude of losses and cost structure just wasn't good enough. Since my previous buy rated coverage, the stock has been on that same downward trend. This past earnings didn't help with the large spend from management, and now there is an increasingly greater risk in buying in now since Bysanti has an upcoming PDUFA date on February 21st. The upside potential from positive approval is clear, but I highlight my areas of concern with the clinical package. The result of this is that shares now look extremely discounted. However, for investors considering buying prior to the decision date, they should understand that approval is far from guaranteed. This is why I have chosen a hold rating.

FY25

Vanda delivered somewhat mixed FY25 earnings. We saw some top-line growth in its core psychiatry franchise, but also heavy losses driven mainly by investment spending and a major one-time tax charge. FY25 revenues rose about 9% to $216.1 million, again for the most part powered by Fanapt, which grew 24% to $117.3 million. It seems prescriptions and new-to-brand demand surged here. This kind of performance goes to show some real commercial momentum in schizophrenia and bipolar I disorder, and we have to give the company's intensified direct-to-consumer and brand investment strategy some credit there. However, we can't ignore the heavy losses, which offset the revenue growth. Operating expenses were significantly higher, particularly in selling, general, and administrative costs and R&D, as Vanda expanded their pipeline and is also preparing for multiple potential launches. As a result, the company posted a hefty FY25 operating loss of $151.2 million and a GAAP net loss of $220.5 million, compared to just $18.9 million in 2024. So that tells us 2025 was a heavy reinvestment year rather than one focused on near-term profitability.

We should also keep in mind that a major distortion in reported results came from a one-time, non-cash valuation allowance of $113.7 million against deferred tax assets. So it would seem management's conclusion here is that future taxable income is no longer sufficiently certain to justify carrying those assets. So if we strip this out, the non-GAAP net loss was $106.8 million, which is still very sizable but a bit more representative of the company's economics. From a balance sheet perspective, Vanda does still remain pretty well-capitalised. They managed to end 2025 with about $263.8 million in cash and marketable securities, which should give them a meaningful runway to fund pipeline programs and, most importantly, see out the upcoming commercial launches of Nereus and potentially Bysanti. Total operating losses in 2025 amounted to $367.3 million, and if we take this as a rough proxy for cash burn, Vanda would appear to have about 8 to 9 months of operational runway at the current burn rate.

Management also gave us a 2026 revenue guidance of about $230 to $260 million from existing marketed products alone, which would suggest more growth even before we see contributions from new approvals. This does put the company at an inflection point, with them being financially strained in the short term due to that aggressive spending, but strategically quite well leveraged to convert their recent regulatory wins/late-stage assets into a more diversified revenue stream.

Upcoming Nereus launch

As you know, Nereus, or tradipitant, is set to be the first new motion-sickness drug in decades. It's a novel oral therapy approved by the FDA for the prevention of vomiting induced by motion. This is actually the first new treatment in this category in over 40 years. As a quick recap, it works by blocking the neurokinin-1 (NK-1) receptor, a receptor in our brain involved in the nausea/vomiting pathway. It needs to be taken orally before any exposure to motion, which sets it up more as a prophylactic option for people who suffer nausea and vomiting from travel. This is a condition affecting a large number of adults in the U.S. So far, the approval is limited to the U.S., and while international regulatory progress hasn't been announced publicly, we can expect the U.S. launch to come sometime this year.

Analysts have actually projected that the treatment could exceed about $100 million in annual U.S. sales at its peak, and that would only be for motion-induced vomiting. Now, some forecasts suggest an even broader opportunity if we see the treatment adopted for GLP-1 weight-loss drug-induced nausea. However, to touch on the extended potential in GLP-1 nausea, I personally don't see too much positivity that they may translate the application into a big commercial win. I would expect most patients to tolerate the mild nausea just fine, and in fact, eating less is part of why they lose weight, so from that perspective, there's limited motivation to add another drug. When medication is actually needed, cheap generics like ondansetron or even promethazine usually work well. Also, just to be clear, Vanda's drug was approved for motion-induced vomiting, not nausea broadly, even if it can be used off-label. So that would make me rather sceptical about assuming large uptake in the GLP-1 population. However, after saying that, some studies have shown promising Phase 2 results, with a Phase 3 program already planned.

However, for 2026 profitability, investors should focus on the contribution we could see from Nereus sales in motion-induced vomiting. It means pricing and insurance coverage details are going to be vital, both of which haven't been finalised yet, but based on industry commentary, I think we can expect a premium pricing model compared to over-the-counter alternatives. This could have both negative and positive effects. As you know, we may see a premium price enhance revenue potential, but it may also be just too pricey for those looking for something while traveling. So an over-the-counter option would be far more attractive in that sense.

Potential of Bysanti

Bysanti or milsaperidone, on the other hand, is a potential new antipsychotic for bipolar disorder and schizophrenia. Bysanti is essentially a new chemical entity that acts as a serotonin-dopamine antagonist. It is actually very closely related to their existing antipsychotic, Fanapt, but with a twist. Vanda has optimised Bysanti for oral administration with some potentially differentiated clinical properties. Its NDA was for the treatment of bipolar I disorder and schizophrenia and is currently under FDA review with an upcoming PDUFA action date of February 21st.

Now, if we see Bysanti approved, I think the drug offering could significantly expand Vanda's psychiatry franchise. It would add a category with a global market estimated at around $20 billion, significantly broadening the company's commercial footprint beyond just Fanapt, an area in which we're seeing sales do well. As for Bysanti, administration would just be oral, once daily. The candidate is also being evaluated as an adjunctive treatment in major depressive disorder (MDD), with results expected much later in 2026. So that puts the near-term catalyst as the PDUFA date on the 21st. Of course, detailed pricing and uptake forecasts aren't yet published, but we typically see antipsychotics command substantial annual revenue streams when they achieve broad market adoption. This is particularly the case when they offer improved tolerability/efficacy compared with existing therapies. I also think that the potential sales opportunity could be very similar to Fanapt, given the similarity of the two. The approval and later a good launch of Bysanti would also support management's long-term growth targets.

PDUFA decision: areas of concern

We have to think of Bysanti as essentially a 'me-too' antipsychotic with no striking clinical differentiation. The molecule was identified as an active metabolite of iloperidone, which is the generic core of Fanapt. This is what quickly interconverts to iloperidone in our bodies, with bioequivalence shown in single- and multiple-dose studies. Now, intuition would lead us to believe that this can be pretty attractive from a development standpoint. After all, if Fanapt can get approval, why can't Bysanti? However, for me, it raises some questions about whether the drug offers meaningful benefits over the parent compound. You see, unlike Fanapt, which faced a long and difficult historical path to initial schizophrenia approval—if you remember, it was originally rejected by the FDA in 2008 before finally being approved in 2009 after additional data—there's no guarantee that merely demonstrating bioequivalence and safety will satisfy current efficacy expectations. Keep in mind it needs to operate in a very crowded class of atypical antipsychotics, where the bar has effectively been raised a lot over the past decade.

Efficacy signal and competitive landscape are mainly supported by bioequivalence studies showing that milsaperidone is equivalent to iloperidone/Fanapt, at both single and multiple doses. Efficacy and safety data rely on historical iloperidone trials, including studies in acute schizophrenia, bipolar I manic/mixed episodes, and schizophrenia relapse prevention, providing a pretty well-characterised efficacy and tolerability profile.

Now, this is similar to Fanapt, which never became a high-usage antipsychotic in practice, which in part was because other drugs with broader real-world advantages were already entrenched. So if the FDA interprets Bysanti's data as offering only incremental benefit over the existing therapies on the market, without a clear safety edge, it could be the case that we see a negative outcome rather than approval. Now, I am not saying this will be the case; it is just something to keep in mind for those thinking of buying this week, prior to the approval. Shares are priced extremely attractively for this very reason; the risk surrounding the stock is very high.

Another area of concern would be safety issues and class scrutiny that we usually see within this space. Antipsychotics face a lot more regulatory focus on metabolic, cardiac (QTc prolongation), and neurological side effects. This is especially true for chronic use in bipolar/schizophrenia populations with comorbidities. If we take a look at current and past FDA reviews, they have been pretty cautious. Fanapt itself is known to carry cardiac safety risks, particularly QTc interval prolongation, which is directly documented in its prescribing information. So even if Bysanti is bioequivalent to a previously approved drug, be aware that legacy doesn't guarantee acceptance. Expect regulators to look at the totality of the evidence in the present, including whether side effect profiles are acceptable relative to established standards.

Now, I frame the above as pretty negative; however, investors will know there are a lot of positives still surrounding the Bysanti clinical package. Firstly, it rests on a well-understood pharmacologic foundation and a historically approvable mechanism. Bysanti being closely related to iloperidone/Fanapt means the agency is not evaluating a novel or unpredictable pathway here but rather a drug with known dopamine and serotonin receptor activity and an established efficacy framework. The clinical data also show statistically significant improvements on standard psychiatric endpoints such as symptom rating scales used in schizophrenia and bipolar disorder. The safety profile also appears broadly consistent with the atypical antipsychotic class, without any new or unexpected toxicities emerging. So from a regulatory standpoint, the FDA tends to favour those packages that demonstrate reproducible efficacy, class-consistent safety, and pharmacologic plausibility, all of which Bysanti appears to deliver going into the decision.

Valuation

When it comes to a valuation, the recent earnings pushing the stock price down have made metrics a lot more attractive. Now, Vanda appears very deeply discounted on a sales and asset basis. There has been stock volatility and earnings losses, and with the risk inherent in the upcoming PDUFA, investors don't seem to be valuing the company too highly. I think the big caution is from the PDUFA decision; the stock has already been on a downward trend, and a negative outcome this week would severely re-rate the stock downwards. Shares now trade at just 1.7x trailing sales and 1.4x forward sales, versus a healthcare sector median near 3.6 to 3.8x. This would imply a discount of more than 50% to peers. Even more striking is when we look at the enterprise value of only about $115 million against the expected 2026 revenues of $230 to $260 million. This gives us an EV to sales multiple of about 0.5x, which places the stock in the top valuation tier on Seeking Alpha relative to the sector. So it's clear the market is heavily discounting execution risk around launches and cash burn rather than the revenue base itself.

On the balance sheet side, the valuation is supported by some substantial liquidity, which is the good news. Vanda holds about $264 million in cash with just minimal debt at $10.91 million. The stock trades near book value at 1.1x price-to-book, compared with a sector median near 2.8x. Elsewhere, earnings-based multiples, of course, remain non-meaningful due to the current losses we're seeing, with long-term consensus estimates showing a return to profitability far later in the decade. Price to earnings is projected to be around the mid-teens by 2029. Overall, the valuation is showing us a market pricing in heavy operational and even heavier regulatory risk. Now, this does offer some asymmetric upside if Nereus and Bysanti contribute meaningfully to growth later, but there are a lot of hurdles to get over first before we can see that outcome. It makes 2026 a very important year for the stock's direction.

Looking ahead

So in conclusion, Vanda stock is very high-risk with the upcoming decision date, but also very high-reward. The upside is that we're seeing strong Fanapt growth with the upcoming launches of Nereus and potentially Bysanti. However, there are heavy losses, aggressive spending, and regulatory uncertainty to consider before buying into a discounted stock. Particularly around Bysanti. They hold substantial cash reserves, providing a runway for near-term operations, but the market is clearly pricing in execution and approval risks rather than their underlying revenue potential. Investors should expect volatility in the near term in either direction, with 2026 set to be a pivotal year for Vanda's trajectory.

data.title: FX Market Awaits North American Leadership
data.description:
The North American market took the dollar (DXY) lower on Tuesday, and there was little follow-through selling. Yesterday, the dollar was bid in North America, and rather than resist, European more than Asia-Pacific operators have extended the greenback’s gains. The somewhat more hawkish FOMC minutes saw the US two-year yield recover to session highs yesterday and a little bit more today. However, near 4.47%, the yield remains below last week’s high (~4.51%) ahead of today’s data.

There are two other large considerations. First, despite the talks, the market is on edge about a possible US strike on Iran. March WTI is up 1.7% today after yesterday’s 4.6% advance. It is near last month’s high (~$66.50). Second, tomorrow is a decision day for the US Supreme Court. It does not pre-announce which cases have been decided, but there is a chance of a verdict on the president’s use of emergency powers to tariff.

Prices

G10

• Yesterday, the euro traded marginally on both sides of Tuesday’s trading range and settled below its low. The bearish outside down day saw the euro fall to almost $1.1780 yesterday. It was unable to resurface above $1.1810 and slipped to nearly $1.1775 in late European morning turnover. The low set earlier this month was around $1.1765, and a break could spur another cent decline.

• The dollar rose almost 1% against the yen yesterday, which is its biggest advance of the month. Recall that it fell every day last week, defying expectations for a weaker yen coming out of the stunning LDP victory and subsequent rally in JGBs. The JPY154.90 area was approached in North America. The gains were extended to almost JPY155.35 before sellers emerged and took the greenback to almost JPY154.60 in the European morning. The intraday momentum indicators are overextended, suggesting limited downside in early North American activity.

• Sterling traded mostly inside Tuesday’s 1 1/3-cent range yesterday, but it traded heavily. It fell to almost $1.3465 today, having been unable to rise above $1.3520. The intraday momentum indicators are stretched, suggesting a bounce in early North American turnover. The next area of support is seen in the $1.3440-60 area.

• The Canadian dollar looks vulnerable. It posted its lowest settlement in nearly two weeks. The greenback pushed against CAD1.37 yesterday as it enjoyed a good bid in the North American afternoon. It rose to CAD1.3705 today but is consolidating in the CAD1.3670-1.3690 area in Europe. The greenback needs to overcome the CAD1.3725 area to signal a move toward CAD1.3760-1.3800.

• The Australian dollar traded quietly and softly yesterday. It spent most of the session confined to about a third of a cent above $0.7050 before breaking down to $0.7035 in the North American afternoon. This held with the help of a robust jobs report today. Still, the market was reluctant to push the Aussie above $0.7080. Meanwhile, a break of $0.6990-0.7000 would signal an outright correction rather than consolidation, with downside potential for 1-1.5 cents.

EM

• The US dollar was sold to nearly MXN17.0865, a new low since mid-2024, in Europe yesterday. It traded below MXN17.10 in early North American turnover, where dollar buyers emerged and set the greenback to MXN17.24. It traded a little above MXN17.25 today. Nearby resistance is seen in the MXN17.27-28 area.

• After strengthening on Monday, the offshore yuan continues to trade quietly in the range set then (~CNH6.8810-6.9060). The dollar traded to CNH7.9040 today, while holding above CNH6.89.

• Foreign investors have bought Indian equities this month but sold nearly twice the amount last month. Equity buying may be helping steady the rupee. The dollar is trading between INR90.07 and INR90.85 this month after reaching a record high late last month near INR92.0165.

Other Markets

• China (SHCOMP), Hong Kong (HSI), and Taiwanese (TWSE) equity markets remain closed for the extended holiday. Japan’s Topix was up more than 1%, while the Nikkei (NKY:IND) lagged. South Korea’s Kospi (KOSPI) jumped 3%. India’s indices are off nearly 1.5%. Europe’s STOXX 600 (STOXX) is off around 0.7% and is threatening to snap a three-day advance. US index futures (SPX) are off 0.25-0.35%.

• Benchmark 10-year yields are mostly higher. Japan’s 10-year bond yield is a little firmer after a mild reception to its 20-year bond sale today. European yields are 1-2 bp higher, and the 10-year US Treasury yield (US10Y) is up a basis point to 4.09%.

• Gold is straddling the $5000 area and is in a narrow range. It settled near $5042 at the end of last week and has stalled today near $5022. Silver is firm and reached a five-day high near $79.50 before consolidating.

• Fear that US and Iran are still edged toward conflict has helped lift March WTI above $66. Recall that Tuesday’s low was slightly below $62. The high from late January was almost $66.50.

Data

• The US reports December trade deficit today. It may surprise some, but through November, the US recorded a larger trade deficit in 2025 than it did in the same period in 2024 (~$872.51 bln vs. $806.58 bln). In fairness, in the seven months after “Liberation Day”, the trade shortfall was reduced to $393.44 bln from $554.49 bln in the May-November 2024 period. The trade (and inventory) data will help economists fine-tune forecasts for Q4 GDP that are released on Friday. The Philadelphia Fed’s February business outlook often sees a market reaction to the headline. It is expected to have softened from the 12.6 reading in January. Weekly jobless claims are due at the same time. A little later, December Leading Economic Indicators are due but have lost whatever heft they may have had given that they have been warning of a recession for some time. The last time the index rose was in November 2024, which itself was the first time since February 2024. Today, the Fed’s Bowman, Bostic, Kashkari, and Goolsbee speak. The implied yield of the December 2026 Fed funds futures is about 3.05%. The current average effective rate, which is what the contract settles at, is 3.64%.

• Canada reports December merchandise trade balance today. In the January-November period, Canada recorded a deficit of about C$30.57 bln, compared with a shortfall of C$8.9 bln in the first 11 months of 2024. In the seven months since the US “Liberation Day”, Canada recorded a trade deficit of almost C$24 bln, compared with a slightly more than C$7bln shortfall in the same period in 2024.

• Minutes from the recent meeting of Mexico’s central bank will be released today. Banxico appears to have signaled an extended pause, but it held out the possibility of resuming the easing cycle later this year.

• With Q4 GDP already reported, the eurozone’s December current account and construction output had minimal impact. The eurozone’s current account surplus narrowed by almost 40% last year. Construction output has yet to register the infrastructure spending. It was largely unchanged last year after a flat 2024.

• After the UK’s jobs and inflation reports over the past two sessions, today’s CBI order book data for February had negligible impact.

• Australia created almost 18k jobs in January. Full-time positions increased by 50.5k after a 56.8k jump in December. The participation rate was steady at 66.7%, which is about 0.6% below the record high set last January. Defying expectations, the unemployment rate held steady at 4.1%. The futures market increased the odds of a May hike to almost 85% from a little less than 80% yesterday.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: La-Z-Boy Is Relaxed And Comfortable Despite Uncomfortable Market Conditions
data.description:
It has only been three months since my initial coverage of La-Z-Boy, Incorporated (LZB). And yet, its value has already risen by over 20% despite my cautious stance and the uncomfortable market situation. Even so, I understand the optimistic view of its investors considering its fundamental soundness and resilience. In fact, it continues to work on improving its supply chain structure while maintaining its domestic market relevance.

LZB Q3 2026: Some Strengths To Withstand Challenges

The market environment remains highly volatile and quite unfavorable for La-Z-Boy, Incorporated. Inflation and tariff woes are some aspects that continue to affect its performance. Yet, its strategic operations reflect its resilience to these headwinds. You can see it in its most recent performance.

During Q3 2026, its sales amounted to $541.6M, up by 3.8% YoY from $521.8M. This contradicted my cautious view of its performance in my previous coverage. If you remember, I expected its Q2 2026 sales to decrease by 1-3%. But lo and behold, it exceeded my expectations and sustained its rebound in Q3. One factor to consider was its strategic store openings and acquisitions to increase the market presence and capture more demand. Note that it has already integrated the 15 acquired Southeast US stores into its operations. Even if you look at its sales per store, LZB improved. On average, LZB generated sales of $1.448M per store ($544.6M/374 stores) versus $1.442M YoY. This means that its expansion remained fruitful.

Of course, LZB is not fully insulated from market challenges. Inflation continued to raise its cost pressures. The operating costs remained manageable. The $18M YoY increase was lower than in sales, with $20M. So, its gross profit margin remained high at over 40%. It also shows that LZB managed its cost sensitivity very well despite the rising prices. The larger increase came from SG&A expenses, which should not be surprising since wages had to increase to cope with inflation. Also, the increased number of stores and the integration of acquired stores raised fixed costs. As a result, the operating margin decreased. Nonetheless, sales were still more than enough to cover all costs and expenses. Hence, it stayed profitable.

Challenges

Stubborn inflation is still an obvious challenge for companies within the consumer discretionary sector. At 2.7%, inflation is still close to its 14-month high of 3.0%. In addition, positive inflation means prices are still generally increasing, which can affect purchasing power and consumer spending. Tariffs may exacerbate the situation since they raise the costs incurred by domestic manufacturers like LZB. As a result, it may pass it on to the domestic consumers, which may soften demand. The impact lies heavily in its cost pressures. That is why its operating costs and expenses increased and easily offset revenue growth. The only consolation is that its revenues remained high and ensured profitability.

Mitigants/Drivers

Despite this, LZB has strengths and risk mitigants to ensure its resilience and longevity.

One aspect to consider is its strategic market positioning. LZB knows its target market very well, supported by its strong pricing power. It capitalizes on value proposition rather than cheaper pricing. LZB's products are unique as it leverages comfort and flexibility to attract customers. We all know how special its seats and recliners are. And since it targets middle-, upper-middle-, and upper-income households, demand is resilient.

Another factor is its strategic business model. This is evident in its vertical integration since over 90% of its products are manufactured in North America, especially Mexico. Also note that it has already closed its manufacturing in the UK, lowering its import reliance further. LZB may also benefit from the USMCA trade deal, so tariffs from these sources may still have preferential rates. Aside from that, Mexico's protectionist shift will help it lock in the US-Mexico-China trade triangle. It can now act as a key manufacturing hub while balancing the trade flow between these two giants. This benefits LZB in the sense that manufacturing and importing from Mexico may not be as costly as initially expected.

Additionally, its sales and distribution channels are well-diversified. Aside from its owned stores, it also has independent sellers/dealers and franchising. So, it has more secure and flexible income streams. It is also increasing its digital exposure, which is crucial as the world transitions to the digital economy.

With respect to its fundamentals, LZB is still strong. Because its operations remain profitable, its operating cash flow also remains high. This is more than enough to cover capex. Their difference, or FCF, is also high enough to cover capex, debt repayments, and share buybacks. So, it's not surprising that it maintains adequate cash of over $300M. This is equivalent to 38%, making it very liquid. This can also cover more than half of all current liabilities.

Lastly, it has zero borrowings. If we consider finance lease liabilities, then cash can cover over 50% of the total. In addition, the Net Debt/EBITDA is only 1.4x, which means that LZB only needs 1.4 years to repay the whole amount if it doesn't have to. In reality this is a short period, which proves its high capacity and capital buffer to sustain its operations and dividends.

Valuation

To reassess LZB's valuation, I updated the Dividend Discount Model. Its annualized dividend is now $0.97 versus $0.88 in my previous coverage because the quarterly payout increased from $0.22 to $0.242. So, the dividend growth rate is also higher at 9.71%. Meanwhile, I still set its risk premium at 6.0% to be conservative and consistent with my previous coverage. But note that the risk-free rate is now 4.3% versus 4.1% before due to the increase in the 10-year treasury yield. Lastly, my estimated Beta is now 1.28. This means that the stock price trend has become less volatile. Using all these numbers, my TP, or target price, is $42.73. If I add a 5% risk consideration, it will be $40.58. This justifies the recent price increase.

Technicals

LZB's technicals have shown price consolidation in the months that followed my previous coverage. But after the release of the Q3 2026 report, the market bid for a lower price as it opened at around $33 and closed at $35. We can also see some weaknesses as the price trends below the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, showing that the current momentum is weaker than in the past 50 and 200 days. Also, the 50-day SMA is starting to flatten and even trend downwards. This shows that market sentiments have already neutralized and weakened compared to Q4 2025.

Despite this, MACD and RSI show some buying opportunities as the stock approaches oversold conditions. The MACD line is decreasing below the signal line amid the decreasing buying volume and increasing selling volume. Meanwhile, the histogram is within negative territory, which implies that selling volume has already outpaced buying volume. Its RSI also shows an increasing selling trend, but volume/index is starting to become too much. This may lead to overselling, which may open a new buying room for investors.

Conclusion

La-Z-Boy, Incorporated remains safe despite volatile market conditions. This is supported by its robust fundamentals and strategic business model. Valuation is already reasonable at this level, which justifies further upside. Meanwhile, technicals have weakened but show new buying opportunities. I decided to upgrade its rating from hold to buy.

data.title: Super Micro Computer: Margins May Expand This Year (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I'm upgrading Super Micro Computer, Inc. (SMCI) to a Strong Buy after the company posted blowout Q2 FY26 results and issued Q3 and full-year guidance that pushed the Street to revise its revenue models significantly higher.

I see the possibility of a near-term shift in the sentiment around this stock. In my view, the pressure on margins that I discussed in my last coverage is now mostly priced in, and the company actually beat EPS expectations in Q2.

Given the 75% drop from the 2024 highs, I think a turnaround is likely if the company succeeds in maintaining the recent growth trajectory and improves its gross margins.

To be clear, I think the bottom line matters more than the top-line growth. The competitive pressures from the likes of Dell, Lenovo, or HPE have led to a deterioration in margins in the AI server space since the start of the AI supercycle, with SMCI being affected the most.

Therefore, the expansion of the DCBBS segment this year (which has a margin of over 20%, as noted in the last earnings call) is the key catalyst to monitor.

From what I'm seeing after Q2 earnings, with the stock relatively flat, I think the growth story matters less than what the bulls think. That said, the figures reported last quarter were very decent. I discuss more below.

Q2 FY26 Highlights

Q2 was a complete blowout quarter. The company reported record revenue of $12.68B, significantly higher than the Street's $10.23B estimate. From a guidance perspective, the company guided over $12.3B in revenue for Q3, vs. the Street's $10.17B projection.

Therefore, I was not surprised in the slightest about the analyst's upward sales revisions after Q2 earnings.

From a growth perspective, the $12.68B last quarter represents a 123% yoy growth, and a 153% sequential increase. Naturally, the company obliterated its own guidance of $10-$11B for the quarter.

Now, a good part of the beat was due to a $1.5B tailwind coming from delayed Q1 shipments. Even though this is a one-off spike, this doesn't mean that growth is going to slow down in the next quarters. Below is the analyst consensus for the next year, showing triple-digit returns in two of the next 4 quarters.

As a side note, the big slowdown in growth projected in FQ2 2027 is likely due to a tough comp because the prior-year quarter (i.e., FQ2 2026) was already at a very high run-rate due to the $1.5B tailwind.

Speaking of comps, over 90% of the Q2 mix was driven by AI GPU platforms, with 86% concentrated in the U.S.

Given the margin pressure (a topic I will discuss in the next section), the high-margin Data Center Building Block Platform was a key topic during the earnings call:

First and foremost, Super Micro undergoes its fourth phase of product evolution with DCBBS as its key focus. As these data center deploys scale, DCBBS is and will become an increasingly important part of our value. In the first half of fiscal year '26, DCBBS solutions accounted for 4% of our profit. We expect this part of our profit to grow and meaningfully contribute to the second half of fiscal '26, and we see that growth accelerated to at least double-digit contribution by end of calendar 2026.

Now, the bottom line of the company doesn't look as bright as the top line, even though the company obliterated EPS expectations last quarter.

Despite that, gross margins tanked to 6.3%, and the guidance for Q3 suggests a very small improvement of 30 bps (sequentially). I discuss more below.

Record Revenue, Low Margin

The explosive AI growth that I noted in the last section comes at a price: low margins.

Specifically, Q2 FY26 gross margin fell to 6.3% vs 9.3% in Q1 and 11.8% a year ago. I considered including below a chart showing the evolution of gross margins since the start of the AI supercycle.

The problem that I see in the AI server space is the fact that the leaders are giving up on margins in order to win large deals. Take the example of Dell below:

Or, to a lesser extent, Hewlett-Packard:

As is often the case, SMCI provided other reasons for the bottom-line pressures, including tariffs and component shortages, along with higher expedite costs from shipping new platforms, as noted by the CFO's comments during the last earnings call:

Gross margins were impacted by customer and product mix as well as higher freight, production and expedite costs, as we began to ship new platforms on a large scale.

The CEO further added:

In Q2 especially, the expedited transportation cost, ongoing component shortages and their volatile pricing, along with tariffs, have impacted our short-term gross margins.

On the customer concentration side, I am not particularly concerned by the fact that one large data center customer was 63% of Q2 revenue. Hyperscalers are projected to spend $650B in CapEx this year, and I don't see any signs to suggest a slowdown or a digestion phase in 2027.

The risk, however, is due to unexpected configuration changes by one of these large customers. Q2 already included $1.5B of delayed Q1 shipments due to design win upgrades to GPU racks. The risk of more unplanned changes is not completely off the table, especially with the mass production of Nvidia's Rubin Vera in H2 this year.

On the risk mitigation side, I think the company may see some margin accretion from the Data Center Building Block Platform this year. So far, DCBBS solutions have accounted for only 4% of the company's profit. However, management plans to increase the contribution of this segment in 2H FY'26 and accelerate to at least a double-digit share of profit by the end of calendar 2026.

From a margin perspective, DCBBS was said to have a net margin of more than 20%, which is materially higher than the corporate average.

Overall, I would keep an eye on the expansion of this segment, especially as the company is moving into transformers, generators, and backup devices. That said, I don't expect any meaningful upside for margins until the back end of the year.

Valuation

On a 1-year forward basis, SMCI is trading at just 17x next year's earnings. Given the exposure to AI, with more than 90% of last quarter's revenue related to AI GPU platforms, this multiple is dirt cheap, in my view.

However, when compared to some of its peers in the AI/GPU server space, SMCI is the cheapest.

As seen above, both Lenovo and Dell are cheaper than SMCI on a 1-year forward P/E basis. Bear in mind that both Dell and Lenovo reported higher gross margins in the last quarter (20.7% and 15.1%, respectively).

Looking Ahead

I think the bull case on SMCI largely depends on the company showing in the next quarter that the growth momentum in Q2 was just the start of a super growth cycle. With Q3 guided above $12.3B versus the Street's $10.17B, and analysts' revenue models jumping after Q2 earnings, I think SMCI is getting close to a sentiment shift. The nearest catalyst I see is Nvidia ramping Rubin Vera into volume production in H2 this year. With $650B in hyperscaler CapEx in the background, that could catch the Street off guard and likely drive another round of upward revenue revisions, similar to what we have seen in Q2.

Another catalyst to turn around the sentiment could be an improvement in gross margins. To be clear, I don't expect them to be at Dell's 20% level any time soon. However, the DCBBS expansion from just 4% of profit in 1H FY'26 to a double-digit profit contribution by the end of calendar 2026 could lift margins. In the last call, this segment was said to have over 20% net margin economics.

From a technical perspective, shares are basically at the $30 support level.

Unless there are more revenue changes at the last moment from some of the major customers, I don't see why shares would trade lower than $30 a share. That said, the market can be irrational, and if there is a near-term correction, there is a risk of breaking below $30.

For this reason, my strong buy rating is looking at a multi-year timeframe.

data.title: Nutanix: Solid FCF Subplot, But Not Very Compelling Otherwise
data.description:
Down In The Dumps

Nutanix, Inc. (NTNX), may be an infrastructure software company that helps enterprises manage their IT infrastructure more efficiently, but it certainly hasn’t been a great source of help for its shareholder base for a while now. Over the past couple of years, we’ve written about this stock thrice (all through which it has underperformed the broader markets), and since our last hold-rated article that came out in late August 2025, NTNX has lost ground even further.

In recent periods, large swathes of the financial press have been quick to sign the death warrant for software companies across the board, and one can see that reflected in the performance of the SPDR S&P Software & Services ETF (XSW), which is down by over 20%, underperforming the broader markets by over 4x; however, our stock in focus has fared even worse than the software universe, witnessing negative returns of almost 40%, which is twice as bad as the peer set.

Meanwhile, since December 2025, NTNX has also been assigned an unsavory quant rating of 1.21, or a strong sell, by Seeking Alpha’s quant system (a tag it had never garnered for the last three years)!

With NTNX seemingly out of favor with the investment community, some hope comes in the form of an earnings event (its Q2 earnings are due to be released post-market on the 25th of February) that could serve as a fitting platform for the company to ameliorate concerns and potentially stoke some positive investor interest. To be clear, we don’t want to come across as myopic, and we recognize that this event also has the potential to make things worse, as was the case the last time NTNX encountered an earnings event (the stock gapped down and closed -18% lower the day after the Q1 earnings in November 2025).

Either way, this looks like a very decisive event, so here are some of the key subplots that investors should be mindful about

What Are the Key Metrics to Watch Out for During Earnings?

Firstly, it’s worth pondering what sort of a beat could potentially invigorate the NTNX share. As we’ve noted previously, NTNX’s delivery vs. consensus expectations has traditionally been near-perfect; in fact, the Q1 (NTNX follows a July-ending fiscal) revenue miss of -0.9% was something of an outlier, and this was only because of a timing issue rather than any lost revenue.

Nonetheless, over the last 4 years, NTNX has been averaging a topline beat of 2.7% per quarter (which would be seen as acceptable), and if one were to also consider the variability of its quarterly beat, we feel a beat of anything over 4.7% will likely be taken very well by the markets, as it would be over 1 standard deviation from the mean.

What also increases the likelihood of a revenue beat is that street expectations have been dialed down for Q2 over the past three months by around -6%. Back in late November, management guided to a Q2 revenue range of $705M-$715M, and consensus is currently budgeting for a slightly lower figure of $709.7M over the midpoint of that range. If NTNX ends up meeting that figure, note that it would also represent the first time in 14 quarters that YoY revenue growth has been anything less than double-digit percentages (implied growth of 8.4%).

Nutanix could potentially also lift sentiment by upgrading Q3 revenue expectations (which at $701M is currently on course to also grow at only single-digit percentages annually), but chances of a meaningful upgrade look low, given the seasonal drop-off between Q2 and Q3. Besides, back in November, management had already confirmed that revenue for FY26 will be pushed to future years. With regard to the bookings signed in Q1, a good chunk of this consists of migrations from VMware Broadcom, where new customers are calling for more flexible migration timelines (in the case of large enterprises, these migrations could take close to a year to complete)

Investors should also keep an eye on the pace of new logo growth; NTNX’s cumulative customer count has been growing at a pace of over 10% YoY for the last three quarters and will most certainly cross the 30K landmark in Q2, but the growth of new logos has been slowing every quarter and only grew by less than 2% in Q1. Don’t be surprised to see NTNX’s new logo growth decline in Q2, as the company has been gradually shifting towards larger average deals and larger enterprises that are more difficult to close.

Long-standing stakeholders of NTNC may also have observed that traditionally, NTNX’s operating margins typically spike in Q2 due to the timing of renewal cycles, but even though NTNX’s margins will improve from what was seen in Q1 (19.7%), the company is still expected to see a pronounced YoY hit of around 360bps (with expectations of 21%). Part of this is also linked to the partner-related sales (with the likes of Dell, Cisco, and Lenovo), who are facing delays in shipping hardware due to supply chain delays, which in turn is also causing a recognition mismatch in terms of sales expenses and sales recognition. The good thing, though, is that operating margins in H2-26 are expected to step up, with management guiding to a FY figure of 21.5% (which would imply H2 margins of 22.6%).

One integral metric that has not been impacted by NTNX’s revenue deferral trends is the quantum of free cash flow generated (because once bookings are invoiced, cash is collected upfront, regardless of license provisioning and deployment timings). After generating FCF of $750M in FY25, NTNX intends to generate FCF of $820M in the current fiscal (which would imply growth of 9% YoY), and traditionally Q2 has been one of the strongest cash flow generating quarters alongside Q4, so don’t be surprised to see an upgrade here, particularly as the DSO of 46 is already well above the mean and could see some downward normalization.

Already on a TTM (trailing 12-month) basis, NTNX offers a pretty decent +6% FCF yield, which is over 4x better than what the stock has traditionally yielded. With $820M of annual FCF on the cards, that yield could expand by another 100bps and cross the 7% mark.

How Are NTNX’s Forward Valuations Now?

NTNX, which has traditionally been priced at forward earnings multiples of closer to 40x, has seen its valuations witness some meaningful de-rating to the tune of 40% and can now be picked up at less than 23x earnings, making it rather appetizing on the surface.

But are those discounted valuations still as compelling as the broader software pack? Well, not quite; more so when you consider that NTNX’s peers offer the same cadence of long-term earnings growth.

The image above shows consensus EPS numbers for NTNX over the next three years, and what we can infer is that this is a business with an average annual earnings growth profile of around 16%. In PEG (price-to-earnings growth) terms, it implies a multiple of 1.4x.

In contrast, take a look at XSW ETF’s valuation-earnings trade-off, which covers 138 other software & services stocks besides NTNX. Basically, this universe also offers similar medium-term earnings growth of 16-17% as NTNX, but yet it can be picked up at a much cheaper (30% discount) earnings multiple of less than 16x (or a PEG of roughly 1x)

To sum up, yes, NTNX has never been this cheap before, but is it the best deal you can get in the software universe? Certainly not! And that’s why we feel that even though NTNX’s relative strength ratio (versus other software peers) is currently perched at a 20% discount to its long-term mean, we don’t see why it should benefit from upward mean reversion.

What Do the Technicals Suggest?

The technicals suggest that NTNX is still largely in the grip of bearish interest, although we may not be too far from an intermediate bottom.

Strong bearish pressure can be gleaned from the fact that the stock is now trading below all its key moving averages of 50, 100, and 200, with the gap with the latter proving to be particularly concerning (close to -37%). The 200DMA is widely seen as the prime barometer that dictates long-term bullish and bearish interest, and for NTNX to make up 37% just to get there will take some doing.

Compounding the issue, we have growing bearish interest, with the total number of shares sold short growing by 17% on a YTD basis to over 11M. If sentiment turns, investors shouldn’t expect a meaningfully short-covering bout, as the days-to-cover ratio is quite low at less than two and a half days, making it quite comfortable for the short-sellers to exit their positions.

While all this is not ideal, investors may also want to widen the lens and take a look at NTNX’s larger time frame monthly chart. Yes, we’ve seen NTNX break down from its ascending channel (something it had leaned on for three years), but one could argue that the selling has gone a tad overboard, at least for the intermediate term, as the stock is now perched at its lower Bollinger Band. Also, while we still have a couple of weeks’ worth of price action that is yet to be reflected, for now, February’s candle appears to be in the shape of a hammer, which typically occurs at the end of a downtrend and suggests a climax. Interestingly, this intermediate climax also appears to be taking shape near a crucial pivot point (highlighted in pink), which served as resistance in 2019 and 2021.

Closing Thoughts

NTNX is in a bad way and could desperately do with a compelling earnings event to help stave off growing negative sentiment. However, just like in Q1, NTNX is likely to face some revenue deferral issues in Q2, while new logo growth may be subdued, and margins too are unlikely to be too stellar (relative to what was seen a year ago). In contrast, NTNX’s free cash flow sub-plot should continue to serve as a shining light (on course for a 7% forward FCF yield), but given that the stock is still trading at above industry valuations and only offering the same degree of long-term earnings growth as its peers, we are not sure there will be a large queue waiting to join this club. Reiterate our hold on NTNX.
